core_idea: Saving protects near-term purchasing power and access, while investing accepts risk in pursuit of future growth.
Confusing the two can expose rent money to market swings or leave long-term goals stuck in low-return cash.
cards.example: An emergency fund and a retirement portfolio serve different jobs even if both are money.
cards.key_takeaway: Match the tool to the time horizon and purpose.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Match the tool to the time horizon and purpose.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Saving versus investing" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: An emergency fund and a retirement portfolio serve different jobs even if both are money.

cards.title: Saving versus investing

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Investing, taxes, insurance, debt, and retirement choices depend on personal circumstances, jurisdiction, risk capacity, and current law; consult qualified professionals for advice.
core_idea: An emergency fund is accessible money for unexpected expenses or income disruption.
It reduces the need to sell long-term investments at bad times or take on high-cost borrowing when an unplanned expense collides with a market downturn.
cards.example: A car repair paid from savings does not force a stock sale during a market drop.
cards.key_takeaway: Financial resilience starts with liquidity for surprises.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Financial resilience starts with liquidity for surprises.

core_idea: Investment risk is the possibility that outcomes differ from what you need or expect.
It includes price swings, inflation, default, liquidity, concentration, behavior, taxes, and fraud, not only losing money today.
cards.example: A safe-looking account can still lose purchasing power if inflation is higher than its yield.
cards.key_takeaway: Risk is multi-dimensional and should be named precisely.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Risk is multi-dimensional and should be named precisely.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A safe-looking account can still lose purchasing power if inflation is higher than its yield.

core_idea: Return is the gain or loss from an investment after income, price changes, and costs.
Expected return is uncertain compensation for taking some kind of risk, not a promise.
cards.example: A stock may pay dividends and rise in price, but either part can disappoint.
cards.key_takeaway: Return is what you hope to earn for accepting uncertainty.

core_idea: Time horizon is when the money is expected to be needed and how flexible or fixed that date actually is, since a firm deadline changes what risks are tolerable.
Longer horizons can absorb some volatility better, while short horizons usually need stability and liquidity.
cards.example: Money for next semester's tuition has a different horizon than money for retirement decades away.
cards.key_takeaway: Time horizon determines how much uncertainty is tolerable.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Time horizon determines how much uncertainty is tolerable.

core_idea: Inflation reduces purchasing power over time, meaning the same amount of money buys fewer goods and services as prices for a typical basket of items rise.
Investing often aims to outpace inflation, but higher expected returns usually come with more uncertainty.
cards.example: A bank balance that grows 1 percent while prices rise 4 percent buys less in real terms.
cards.key_takeaway: The goal is future purchasing power, not just a bigger number.

review_prompts.answer_hint: The goal is future purchasing power, not just a bigger number.

core_idea: Compounding happens when investment returns are reinvested and begin earning further returns of their own, so growth builds on growth rather than on the original amount alone.
Time, contribution consistency, costs, taxes, and volatility all affect how compounding works in practice.
cards.example: Interest left in an account can become part of the next period's interest calculation.
cards.key_takeaway: Compounding rewards time and consistency, but it is not magic.

core_idea: Diversification spreads exposure across investments so one failure does not dominate the whole plan.
It cannot remove market-wide risk, but it can reduce avoidable concentration risk.
cards.example: Owning many companies through a broad fund is less dependent on one employer or industry.
cards.key_takeaway: Diversification is humility turned into portfolio design.

core_idea: An index fund seeks to track a market index rather than pick individual winners.
It offers broad diversification and typically low costs, but it still carries the risk of the market it tracks.
cards.example: A total stock market index fund rises and falls with the broad stock market.
cards.key_takeaway: Indexing buys a market slice instead of a prediction about one winner.

core_idea: ETFs and mutual funds pool many investors' money together so each investor owns a proportional share of a diversified basket of stocks, bonds, or other securities.
They differ in trading mechanics, pricing, taxes, and minimums, so the wrapper matters even when holdings are similar.
cards.example: An S&P 500 ETF and mutual fund may hold similar stocks but trade differently during the day.
cards.key_takeaway: Funds are baskets; the wrapper shapes how you buy and hold them.

core_idea: Fees are the costs paid to own, trade, manage, advise on, or administer investments, and they are charged whether or not the underlying investment performs well.
Small-looking percentages can compound against the investor over long periods, since money paid out in fees each year no longer remains invested to grow further.
cards.example: A 1 percent annual advisory fee may seem tiny but can consume a meaningful share of decades of returns.
cards.key_takeaway: Costs are one of the few investment variables you can often see clearly.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Costs are one of the few investment variables you can often see clearly.

core_idea: An expense ratio is the annual operating cost of a fund expressed as a percentage of assets.
It is deducted directly inside the fund's reported returns, so investors rarely see a separate bill and can easily overlook how much the cost is reducing their gains.
cards.example: A fund with a 0.05 percent expense ratio costs far less than one charging 1 percent for similar exposure.
cards.key_takeaway: Expense ratios quietly reduce the return investors keep.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Expense ratios quietly reduce the return investors keep.

core_idea: Liquidity is how quickly and reliably an asset can be turned into spendable money without a major price concession.
Illiquid assets may be valuable but unsuitable for near-term or emergency needs.
cards.example: A house can be valuable and still hard to sell quickly at a fair price.
cards.key_takeaway: Liquidity is about usable access, not just value.

core_idea: Volatility is the degree to which an investment's price swings up and down over time, whether daily, monthly, or across full market cycles.
It is uncomfortable but not automatically the same as permanent loss; context and horizon matter.
cards.example: A broad stock fund may fall sharply in a month and still fit a decades-long plan.
cards.key_takeaway: Volatility is movement; risk is whether movement harms your goal.

core_idea: Market timing is the attempt to move money in and out of investments right before prices rise or fall, essentially trying to predict short-term direction.
It is hard because decisions require being right about direction, timing, taxes, costs, and re-entry under uncertainty.
cards.example: Selling after a scary headline may avoid a drop or miss a rebound, and you only know later.
cards.key_takeaway: Consistently timing markets is much harder than it feels in hindsight.

core_idea: Dollar-cost averaging invests fixed amounts on a schedule, buying more shares when prices are lower and fewer when higher.
It can reduce regret and automate behavior, though it is not a guarantee of better returns than investing a lump sum.
cards.example: A monthly retirement contribution buys at many different market levels.
cards.key_takeaway: Scheduled investing is mainly a behavior and cash-flow tool.

core_idea: Rebalancing brings a portfolio back toward its target allocation after market moves.
It controls risk drift by selling some overweight assets or directing new money to underweight assets.
cards.example: If stocks rise from 60 percent to 75 percent of a portfolio, rebalancing can restore the intended risk mix.
cards.key_takeaway: Rebalancing keeps the plan from being rewritten by market movement.

Taxable and tax-advantaged accounts can hold similar assets but differ in taxes, limits, access, and reporting.

core_idea: Retirement accounts are policy-created containers intended to encourage long-term saving.
Rules about contributions, withdrawals, taxes, penalties, and eligibility are specific and can change, so primary guidance matters.
cards.example: In the United States, a traditional account defers taxes until withdrawal while a Roth account is funded with after-tax money; other countries use different retirement-account rules.
cards.key_takeaway: Retirement accounts are powerful wrappers with rulebooks.

core_idea: An employer match is extra compensation contributed when an employee contributes under plan rules.
It can be valuable, but vesting, limits, investment choices, and cash-flow needs still matter.

core_idea: Risk tolerance is emotional comfort with uncertainty, while risk capacity is the financial ability to absorb bad outcomes.
A sound plan considers both because bravery cannot create time or cash that a goal lacks.
cards.example: Someone comfortable with volatility still should not invest next month's rent in stocks.
cards.key_takeaway: Willingness and ability to take risk are different.

core_idea: Behavioral mistakes happen when fear, greed, overconfidence, or neglect override the plan.
The damage often comes from buying high, selling low, chasing stories, or ignoring costs.
cards.example: An investor who sells every downturn may turn temporary volatility into permanent loss.
cards.key_takeaway: Your behavior can matter as much as your product choice.

core_idea: Herding is following what others appear to be doing instead of evaluating fit, risk, and evidence.
Crowds can sometimes reveal useful information through collective behavior, but the same dynamic can also amplify bubbles, panics, and scams far beyond their initial size.
cards.example: A friend group all buying a hot token does not prove it belongs in your plan.
cards.key_takeaway: Popularity is not proof of suitability.

core_idea: Loss aversion means that losing a given amount of money often feels more painful than gaining that same amount feels good, distorting how risks are weighed.
It can make investors sell after declines, avoid useful risk, or hold losers to avoid admitting loss.
cards.example: A 10 percent drop may feel urgent even when the long-term goal has not changed.
cards.key_takeaway: Emotions around loss need pre-made rules.

core_idea: Investment scams use trust, urgency, secrecy, complexity, or guaranteed-return claims to bypass judgment.
Fraud prevention starts by slowing down, verifying registration, understanding custody, and refusing pressure.
cards.example: A stranger promising safe monthly returns far above market rates is selling a warning sign.

core_idea: A fiduciary is required to act in another person's best interest within the scope of the relationship.
Not every financial professional has the same obligations, compensation model, or conflicts.
cards.example: Asking how an adviser is paid can reveal incentives behind recommendations.
cards.key_takeaway: Advice quality depends partly on duties and incentives.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Advice quality depends partly on duties and incentives.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Asking how an adviser is paid can reveal incentives behind recommendations.

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Investing, taxes, insurance, debt, and retirement choices depend on personal circumstances, jurisdiction, risk capacity, and current law; consult qualified professionals for advice.
core_idea: Robo-advisors are automated platforms that use software and algorithms to recommend or manage investment portfolios based on the inputs a user provides.
They can lower costs and automate rebalancing, but the output is only as appropriate as the assumptions and information entered.
cards.example: A questionnaire may not capture unstable income or upcoming major expenses.
cards.key_takeaway: Automation can help execution but does not remove judgment.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Automation can help execution but does not remove judgment.

connections.label: Builds on Retirement accounts at a high level.

core_idea: A target-date fund holds a diversified mix that becomes more conservative as a chosen date approaches.
It simplifies allocation but still requires checking costs, assumptions, holdings, and whether the date matches the goal.
cards.example: A 2060 fund is generally designed for a later retirement horizon than a 2030 fund.
cards.key_takeaway: Target-date funds package an allocation path, not a personalized guarantee.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Target-date funds package an allocation path, not a personalized guarantee.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A 2060 fund is generally designed for a later retirement horizon than a 2030 fund.

connections.label: Prepares for Credit risk in bonds.

core_idea: Bond prices generally move opposite interest rates because older fixed payments become more or less attractive.
Longer-duration bonds usually react more strongly to rate changes than shorter-duration bonds.
cards.example: If new bonds pay higher interest, an older low-rate bond may need a lower price to compete.
cards.key_takeaway: Bond safety depends partly on rate sensitivity.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Bond safety depends partly on rate sensitivity.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Bonds and interest rates" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: If new bonds pay higher interest, an older low-rate bond may need a lower price to compete.

Prepares for Recessions and staying with a plan.

core_idea: Credit risk in bonds is the chance that the issuer cannot make promised payments.
Higher yields may compensate investors for taking on this risk, but an unusually high yield can also be a warning signal that the market doubts full repayment.
cards.example: A distressed company's bond may offer a high yield because investors doubt full repayment.
cards.key_takeaway: Yield is not free money; it often points to risk.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Yield is not free money; it often points to risk.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Credit risk in bonds" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A distressed company's bond may offer a high yield because investors doubt full repayment.

cards.title: Credit risk in bonds

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Investing, taxes, insurance, debt, and retirement choices depend on personal circumstances, jurisdiction, risk capacity, and current law; consult qualified professionals for advice.
core_idea: A real return adjusts investment performance for inflation, showing growth in actual purchasing power rather than in the nominal dollar amount alone.
It matters because goals are funded with purchasing power, not nominal account statements alone.
cards.example: A 5 percent nominal gain with 3 percent inflation is roughly a 2 percent real gain before other costs.
cards.key_takeaway: Real return tells you what your money can buy.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Real return tells you what your money can buy.
review_prompts.prompt: In your own words, explain "Real returns" and name one way someone could misunderstand it.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A 5 percent nominal gain with 3 percent inflation is roughly a 2 percent real gain before other costs.

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Investing, taxes, insurance, debt, and retirement choices depend on personal circumstances, jurisdiction, risk capacity, and current law; consult qualified professionals for advice.
core_idea: Concentration risk appears when too much depends on one company, sector, country, employer, or asset type.
It can feel comfortable when the concentrated asset is familiar, but familiarity does not remove risk.
cards.example: Holding employer stock and relying on that employer for salary links job and portfolio risk.
cards.key_takeaway: Too much of one thing can quietly dominate the plan.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Too much of one thing can quietly dominate the plan.

core_idea: International diversification spreads exposure across economies, currencies, sectors, and political environments.
It adds its own currency and governance risks while reducing dependence on one home market.
cards.example: A portfolio holding only domestic companies may miss industries or growth patterns elsewhere.
cards.key_takeaway: Global diversification trades home comfort for broader exposure.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Global diversification trades home comfort for broader exposure.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A portfolio holding only domestic companies may miss industries or growth patterns elsewhere.

core_idea: Dividends are company cash distributions to shareholders, usually from profits or reserves.
They are part of total return but not guaranteed, and a high yield can reflect a falling price or stressed business.
cards.example: A company may cut its dividend if earnings weaken.
cards.key_takeaway: Dividends are cash flows, not automatic evidence of safety.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Dividends are cash flows, not automatic evidence of safety.

core_idea: A capital gain happens when an investment is sold for more than its purchase price.
Tax rules, holding periods, account type, and realized versus unrealized gains affect the outcome.
cards.example: A stock can show an unrealized gain on screen before any taxable sale occurs.
cards.key_takeaway: Gains matter differently before and after they are realized.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Gains matter differently before and after they are realized.

core_idea: Taxes can change what investors keep through income, dividends, gains, account rules, and reporting.
Because tax law is jurisdiction-specific and changes, general education should not become personal tax advice.
cards.example: Selling a fund in a taxable account may have different consequences than selling inside a retirement account.
cards.key_takeaway: Tax awareness is necessary, but individual tax decisions need current guidance.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Tax awareness is necessary, but individual tax decisions need current guidance.

core_idea: Insurance transfers defined risks, while investing accepts risk for potential growth.
Some products combine features, but confusing protection with growth can hide costs and tradeoffs.
cards.example: Term life insurance and an index fund solve different problems.
cards.key_takeaway: Protection products and investment products have different jobs.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Protection products and investment products have different jobs.

core_idea: Paying down debt and investing new money both affect future net worth, risk exposure, and financial flexibility, even though they can feel like separate decisions.
The comparison depends on interest rate, tax treatment, liquidity, employer match, stress, and personal constraints.
cards.example: Paying a high-interest credit-card balance can be a clearer return than chasing uncertain investments.
cards.key_takeaway: Debt decisions belong in the same map as investing decisions.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Debt decisions belong in the same map as investing decisions.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Paying a high-interest credit-card balance can be a clearer return than chasing uncertain investments.

cards.boundary_note: Educational only. Investing, taxes, insurance, debt, and retirement choices depend on personal circumstances, jurisdiction, risk capacity, and current law; consult qualified professionals for advice.
core_idea: Goals and buckets separate money by purpose, time horizon, and required certainty.
This reduces the temptation to use one single risky portfolio for every need, since money needed soon should not be exposed to the same swings as money needed decades later.
cards.example: A travel fund, emergency fund, and retirement account can each have different investments or savings tools.
cards.key_takeaway: Clear goals keep portfolios from becoming vague piles of money.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Clear goals keep portfolios from becoming vague piles of money.

core_idea: An investment policy statement is a written rule set for goals, allocation, contributions, rebalancing, and behavior during stress.
It is useful because calm decisions can guide future moments of fear or excitement.
cards.example: A rule to rebalance twice a year can prevent headlines from becoming the strategy.
cards.key_takeaway: A written plan protects the investor from improvising under emotion.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A written plan protects the investor from improvising under emotion.

core_idea: Performance charts show selected time periods, scales, fees, benchmarks, and assumptions.
They can educate or mislead depending on what is included and what is quietly omitted.
cards.example: A chart starting at the bottom of a crash may make returns look smoother than a full-cycle view.
cards.key_takeaway: Always ask what a performance chart chose not to show.

review_prompts.answer_hint: Always ask what a performance chart chose not to show.

core_idea: Past performance describes what already happened to an investment or fund, not what is guaranteed or even likely to happen again in the future.
Markets change because prices already reflect expectations, competition, rates, and new information.
cards.example: A fund that beat peers for five years can still lag afterward.
cards.key_takeaway: History is evidence, not a guarantee.

core_idea: Recessions can bring job risk, market declines, credit stress, and emotional pressure at the same time.
A resilient plan considers emergency cash, allocation, debt, and rules before panic arrives.
cards.example: Someone with a sufficient emergency fund may avoid selling long-term investments after losing income.
cards.key_takeaway: A plan is most valuable when conditions are bad.

core_idea: Professional guidance can be important when stakes, complexity, taxes, legal issues, disability, inheritance, debt, or conflicting goals are significant.
The goal is not to outsource all thinking but to know when general education is insufficient.
cards.example: A cross-border inheritance is not a beginner blog problem.
cards.key_takeaway: Knowing the boundary of self-education is part of good financial judgment.

core_idea: A beginner's system connects emergency savings, time horizon, diversification, costs, behavior rules, and fraud avoidance.
It should be boring enough to follow consistently and explicit enough in its rules to resist hype, headlines, and the pull of chasing whatever is currently popular.
cards.example: A one-page checklist can say what money is for, where it sits, how risk is limited, and when to get help.
cards.key_takeaway: A simple investment system beats scattered product chasing.

review_prompts.answer_hint: A simple investment system beats scattered product chasing.
